symptoms.symptom_name: Completely asymptomatic plaque (no pain, itch or burning)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Extragenital lichen sclerosus is characteristically asymptomatic.
Necrotic and vasculitic lesions are typically painful or tender.
Cellulitis is painful and tender; a painless lesion argues strongly against it.
Porcelain-white atrophic plaques with wrinkled 'cigarette-paper' surface are the hallmark of lichen sclerosus.
Morphea is typically indurated and thickened rather than superficially atrophic and wrinkled.

symptoms.symptom_name: Plaque superficially atrophic, not deeply indurated or bound to underlying fascia

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Deep induration bound to fascia is the discriminating feature of morphea; its absence favours lichen sclerosus.
Superficial atrophy without deep binding fits lichen sclerosus.

symptoms.symptom_name: Blanching reactive erythema around the plaque, appearing only after vigorous rubbing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Blanching erythema temporally linked to rubbing is mechanical, not infective.
Cellulitic erythema is warm, tender and non-blanching, and is not induced by rubbing.

symptoms.symptom_name: No warmth, no purulence, no regional lymphadenopathy on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of warmth, pus and lymphadenopathy argues strongly against active infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: No ulceration, no eschar, no broken or bleeding skin; normal capillary refill in surrounding tissue

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No eschar, ulceration or capillary-refill abnormality makes necrosis/vasculitis very unlikely.
Absence of fever argues against significant infection.
A well, afebrile patient with an isolated skin finding is unlikely to have active systemic vasculitis.

symptoms.symptom_name: No active scaling advancing erythematous border

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A raised scaling active border is expected in tinea; its absence argues against it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe situational health anxiety and fixation on the lesion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Catastrophic misinterpretation of a benign finding, with compulsive checking and rubbing, defines the secondary anxiety component driving the presentation.

symptom_clusters.cluster_name: Benign atrophic plaque in a well patient
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Asymptomatic porcelain-white atrophic plaque with no ulceration, warmth, pus or systemic upset is a benign picture that excludes the feared emergencies.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Palpation distinguishes atrophic lichen sclerosus from indurated morphea; appropriately a routine GP/dermatology task, not an emergency one.
Confirms lichen sclerosus and excludes morphea where the diagnosis is uncertain; done on a routine outpatient timeline.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Emergency bloods / inflammatory markers / imaging for necrosis or sepsis
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: No clinical features of infection, necrosis or systemic illness — ordering an emergency infective/necrosis work-up would be an over-investigation driven by the patient's anxiety.
No scaling active border and no pruritus — tinea is not clinically suggested, so routine antifungal testing is unnecessary.
